description: v0.3 multi-student seed (U13, Plan 001 v3). Replaces the v0.1 1-student × 8-reflection seed (now archived at test/ablation/fixtures/_archive/seed-corpus.json). demo-a is Alice (Sec 2) — the canonical MyWorld design-spec demo student (advisor-plans/context/myworld-demo-transcripts.md), with 9 Singlish reflections, seeded moods, curated VIPS pages/timeline, and a 5-pathway trajectory. demo-b/c/d remain the original hand-curated SG secondary/JC background students with distinct emerging VIPS profiles (different Values dominance, different RIASEC tilt, different Skills evident). Each student has ≥3 distinct context_type values across 7-9 reflections, and a mix of positive + ordinary + negative experiences. Coverage matrix per student is committed as the `coverage_matrix` field inside each student record so AE6 is mechanically verifiable (downstream db.test.ts can read it and assert ≥3 context types per student).

profile.notes_for_review: Bubbly, curious, slow to open up to strangers, warm with friends. Working-class family (dad drives Grab, mum does catering). Physics clicks because Ms Lim ties it to real demos — the 'oh that's why' feeling. St John's Brigade reluctantly; resents foot drills, proud of CPR (thinks of her grandfather). Coming off a failed Standard First Aid badge (froze in the CPR scenario) which dented her usual composure under pressure. Open threads: NCOC (leaning on whether friend Jaya signs up), a Beyblade SIL project her form teacher Mr Lim encouraged, a crush from another school met via walkathon planning, and the JC-vs-poly subject-combo decision. Emerging pattern: drawn to hands-on learning and to understanding how things work under the surface. The negative entry (badge failure) must stay ungeneralized: she's disappointed, not defeated.

students.coverage_matrix: context_type coverage for demo-a (Alice): civic (r1, r2, r3, r4), peer (r5), school (r6, r7, r9), hobby (r8). 4 of 5 enum values; ≥3 satisfied. Affect spread: positive (r2, r5, r7, r8), ordinary (r1, r6), negative (r3), mixed (r4, r9). Initiation: teacher-initiated (r6, r7), self-initiated (rest). Moods: anger r1, joy r2/r5/r7/r8, sadness r3, anxiety r4/r9, fear r6.

reflections.transcript:
Student: eh todays drill so long sia. marching for what i dont get it

Kira: what happened

Student: stand there 40min just turn left turn right. sergeant major say our turns not sharp. i was like ok and then

Kira: did he say why it matters

Student: no lor just say discipline discipline. i get discipline for CPR. this one i dunno leh

Kira: [readback] sounds like the marching felt pointless today, not because you mind discipline, but because you can't see what it's building toward, unlike CPR where the point is obvious. fair?

Student: ya thats it lah
reflections.validation: You sat with the part that felt pointless instead of just complaining and moving on. Wanting to know what the marching builds toward is a fair question, not a discipline problem.
reflections.inferred_meaning: Effort lands for you when you can see what it is building toward — CPR has an obvious why, the drills don't yet.
reflections.story_reframe: Forty minutes of turns felt pointless today — not because you mind discipline, you take CPR seriously — but because nobody could say what the marching was for. The frustration points at something steadier: you work best when the why is visible.
reflections.title: Foot drills frustration

reflections.transcript:
Student: today we learned cpr compressions properly. like the proper depth and rate and everything

Kira: how'd it go

Student: actually quite fun? like everyone was eww you have to do mouth to mouth on the mannequin lah disgusting like that. but i was thinking actually if i ever need to do this for my grandparents at home i would be quite glad i know how

Kira: that's a different way to think about it than your friends were

Student: ya cos like my ah gong heart not that good already, my mum always worried. so when we were doing the compressions i was counting in my head and thinking eh if this really happens one day i actually know what to do now

Kira: did that change how seriously you took the training

Student: definitely. like the instructor was saying push hard push fast, dont be scared to break a rib even, better than not pushing hard enough. i was like ok i need to remember this properly, not just pass the test and forget

Kira: what part do you think you'd actually remember if it really happened

Student: probably the rate. they taught us a song to keep the beat, i think i can still hum it now. the rest i think will come back if i actually have to do it, like muscle memory maybe

Kira: [readback] today's CPR session landed differently for you than for your friends. they were grossed out, you were thinking about your grandfather and being ready to help him if needed, and that gave the training more weight. you're holding onto the compression rhythm especially. anything to add before i save this

Student: nah thats about right
reflections.validation: While your friends were joking about the mannequin, you were counting compressions for your ah gong. You turned a class exercise into something you intend to keep.
reflections.inferred_meaning: Training matters to you when someone real is behind it — you learn for use, not just to pass.
reflections.story_reframe: CPR class landed differently for you than for your friends. You were thinking about your grandfather's heart, so the depth, the rate, the beat-keeping song all got filed under things you might really need — not things on the test.
reflections.title: CPR training and her grandfather

reflections.transcript:
Student: failed my badge today

Kira: oh. what happened

Student: dunno. just failed lor

Kira: which part of it

Student: the cpr scenario one

Kira: what happened during it

Student: i dont know. i just froze i think

Kira: froze how, like forgot what to do, or something else

Student: the patient was like acting distressed and shouting and i didnt know what to do first. i think i missed checking the scene safety also. examiner just said cannot, fail

Kira: that's a lot happening at once. which part felt the worst in the moment, the freezing or the scene safety thing

Student: i think the freezing. like i trained so many times already and then real scenario i just blank

Kira: have you had that happen in other things before, not just first aid

Student: ...not really. usually im ok under pressure. this one just got to me sia

Kira: so this felt different from how you usually are

Student: ya. dunno why. maybe cos got outside examiner watching, made it feel more real or something

Kira: [readback] you failed the CPR scenario part of your badge today, you froze when the simulated casualty got distressed and think you missed a scene safety check too. the freezing bothered you more than the technical miss, especially since you don't usually freeze under pressure, and having an external examiner watching made it feel higher stakes. does that sound right

Student: ya. gonna have to redo next year now

Kira: noted that too. anything else you want in there

Student: no thats it
reflections.validation: You trained hard and still froze when the casualty started shouting — that gap between practice and the real scenario stings more than the result itself.
reflections.inferred_meaning: The freeze bothers you more than the fail, because it doesn't match how you usually are under pressure.
reflections.story_reframe: You failed the CPR scenario of your Standard First Aid badge: a distressed casualty, a missed scene-safety check, an examiner watching. What stays with you isn't the grade — it's freezing when you usually don't. One bad day, with a retry next year.
reflections.title: Failing the Standard First Aid badge

reflections.transcript:
Student: my OC asked if i want to sign up for the zone NCOC course

Kira: what do you think

Student: dunno leh. like half of me wants to try, half dont

Kira: what's the half that wants to try thinking

Student: like i did ok in the cca so far even though i failed the badge thing. ncoc is like a level up i guess, more leadership stuff. could be good

Kira: and the half that doesn't want to

Student: i just remember how much i complained about foot drills and discipline stuff. ncoc probably got even more of that. dont know if i can tahan more of it

Kira: is there anything that would tip it one way

Student: actually ya. my friend jaya also thinking about it. if she goes i think i go also. easier la with someone i know

Kira: so right now it's less about whether you want it and more about whether she's doing it

Student: ...ya i guess thats true huh. didnt think of it like that

Kira: [readback] you're torn on NCOC. the leadership side appeals to you given how the CCA's gone so far, but the discipline-heavy parts you've struggled with before make you hesitant, and right now your decision is leaning on whether jaya signs up too. sound right

Student: ya thats about it. need to ask her first i think
reflections.validation: You laid out both halves honestly — the pull of the level-up and the memory of the drills — and you noticed mid-sentence that the decision is currently resting on Jaya.
reflections.inferred_meaning: Right now the NCOC question is less about wanting it and more about not walking in alone.
reflections.story_reframe: You're torn on NCOC: the leadership side appeals, the discipline-heavy culture doesn't, and the tiebreaker has quietly become whether Jaya signs up. Naming that out loud was new — the next step you set yourself is asking her.
reflections.title: NCOC decision

reflections.transcript:
Student: ok so the walkathon planning meeting today was actually really fun. like i was so reluctant when teacher first asked me to help organize but today i actually enjoyed it

Kira: what made it fun

Student: we had to coordinate with other schools right, so there were students from like four other secondary schools there. and theres this girl from one of the other schools, she's actually super organized like she had a whole spreadsheet already for the route logistics and i was like wow ok

Kira: sounds like she stood out to you

Student: ya lah ok fine i admit i kind of have a crush on her now. dont laugh

Kira: not laughing. what's she like beyond the spreadsheet

Student: she's quite funny actually, dry humor, she made this joke about how our school's route always somehow ends up the longest one and everyone laughed. and she actually listens when people talk, like she'll remember small things you said earlier in the meeting

Kira: did you guys talk much one on one

Student: a bit. we were both in charge of the hydration point planning so we had to discuss together. i kept trying to think of stuff to ask her but everything i thought of sounded lame in my head so i didnt say half of it

Kira: what kind of stuff did you want to ask

Student: like what school she's from, what cca she does, normal things. but somehow felt awkward to ask straight up

Kira: is there a next meeting where you'd see her again

Student: ya next month, theres a walk through of the route before the actual day. so i guess i have another chance

Kira: how are you feeling about that

Student: nervous but also looking forward to it lah. like the actual organizing work also genuinely interesting, i didnt expect that. i used to think this kind of stuff was just admin work but theres actually a lot of coordination and problem solving in it

Kira: so two things happened today then, you found the work itself more interesting than you expected, and there's this whole other thing with her

Student: ya basically. didnt expect either tbh

Kira: [readback] today's walkathon meeting surprised you on two fronts. the organizing work turned out more interesting and hands-on than you assumed, and you've developed a crush on a girl from another school who you worked with on hydration point planning, she's organized, funny, and a good listener, and you're looking forward to seeing her again at next month's route walkthrough even though you felt a bit tongue-tied today. anything to change

Student: nah thats accurate lol
reflections.validation: Two surprises in one afternoon: the organizing work turned out to be real problem-solving, and someone from another school turned out to be worth being tongue-tied around.
reflections.inferred_meaning: Work you wrote off as admin got interesting once you were inside it — and the crush is its own separate discovery.
reflections.story_reframe: You went into walkathon planning reluctant and came out having enjoyed the coordination itself — hydration points across four schools read as a puzzle, not paperwork. There's also someone from another school with a spreadsheet and dry humour; next month's route walkthrough suddenly matters more.
reflections.title: Walkathon planning and a crush

reflections.transcript:
Teacher prompt: Welcome back to school, and hope you had fun at the camp, taking some time out of our normal schooling routine. To maximize your learning, think back to the camp. What were some of the things that stuck with you since?

Student: camp was alright lah. tiring but ok

Kira: what's something from it that's still in your head even now

Student: actually the tchoukball thing keeps coming back to me. my friend joseph twisted his ankle quite badly during the game

Kira: what do you remember about that moment

Student: it happened so fast. he was just running for the ball and then suddenly he was on the ground holding his ankle and everyone kind of froze for a sec before the facilitators ran over

Kira: what was going through your head right then

Student: i think i was just worried for him, like is it broken, can he walk. and then after the facilitators handled it i was thinking damn i actually dont know what to do in that kind of situation, like injury thats not cpr related

Kira: has that thought stuck with you since

Student: ya a bit. like i know cpr now from cca but this was different, more like sports injury kind of thing. made me realize theres other stuff i dont know how to handle

Kira: anything else from camp that's stayed with you, or mostly this

Student: mostly this one. rest of camp was just normal team games and stuff, fun in the moment but nothing i keep thinking about after

Kira: [readback] out of everything at camp, what's stuck with you most is joseph twisting his ankle during tchoukball, you remember how fast it happened and being worried for him, and it left you noticing there's a whole category of injury stuff, beyond CPR, that you wouldn't know how to handle. fair?

Student: ya thats it
reflections.validation: Out of a whole camp, the moment that stayed is the one where you didn't know how to help. That's you taking your own readiness seriously.
reflections.inferred_meaning: You measure yourself by whether you'd know what to do when it matters — and you just found an edge of that map.
reflections.story_reframe: Joseph's ankle is the piece of camp you can't put down: how fast it happened, the worry, and then the quieter thought — CPR training doesn't cover this. You walked away with a gap you now know exists.
reflections.title: Sec 2 Camp

reflections.transcript:
Teacher prompt: Today you attended our school's annual career fair. What were 3 things that stood out to you and why?

Student: ok 3 things. first one, theres this whole industry for making artificial flavors and fragrances, like the people who make the chip flavors and shampoo smells. never knew that was a job

Kira: what made that one stand out

Student: just didnt know it existed lah. like i thought food was either you cook it or some factory just makes it, didnt think there was a whole science behind the flavor part

Kira: what's the second thing

Student: one of the booth people, turns out he used to write for mothership and he went to my school. that was kind of cool, like oh a senior actually did something like that

Kira: did that change how you think about people from your school

Student: a bit, like i guess i never really thought about what alumni end up doing, this one just made it feel more real, like ok people from here actually go do interesting stuff after

Kira: and the third

Student: the physiotherapist talk. he does sports injury rehab. he had his hair gelled back, studied in australia, just seemed like a cool guy honestly

Kira: what part of the talk stuck with you most

Student: how he explained the recovery process, like why certain exercises come before others depending on the injury

Kira: did that connect to anything you've seen recently

Student: hmm. ...oh wait. actually ya, joseph twisted his ankle at camp, i remember thinking after that i didnt know what to do for that kind of thing. and now hearing the physio talk i was like oh thats probably what joseph needs to be doing now, the exercises and stuff

Kira: you put that together pretty quickly just now

Student: ya, didnt realize it until you asked actually. but it makes sense now, like the camp thing and the career fair thing are actually connected for me

Kira: out of all three, which one are you still thinking about the most

Student: probably the physio one. partly cos the job sounds cool, but also cos of the joseph thing now that i think about it

Kira: [readback] three things stood out today: the flavor and fragrance industry, which surprised you that it existed at all, an alumnus who used to write for mothership, which made school feel more connected to real outcomes, and the physiotherapist, whose talk on injury recovery you ended up linking to joseph's ankle from camp, even though you hadn't connected those two moments until just now. the physio one is sitting with you the most. sound right

Student: ya thats exactly it
reflections.validation: You walked in for a school event and walked out with a connection you made yourself — the physio's recovery logic snapped onto Joseph's ankle from camp.
reflections.inferred_meaning: The 'oh that's why' feeling showed up again — this time about how bodies recover, and it linked two separate weeks of your life.
reflections.story_reframe: Three things stood out to you at the career fair: a whole science behind chip flavours, a Mothership writer from your own school, and a physiotherapist whose talk on recovery sequencing suddenly explained what Joseph's ankle needs. The physio one is still sitting with you.
reflections.title: ECG Career Fair

reflections.transcript:
Student: bro mr lim caught us playing beyblade in class today and i thought confirm kena scolded

Kira: what happened instead

Student: he came over and i was like preparing to apologize already, but he just asked if we wanted to do something beyblade related for our student initiated learning project. i was so shocked lor

Kira: what was your first thought when he said that

Student: like... wait we can do that? i thought sil project had to be like serious topic, recycling or community service kind of thing. didnt think beyblade count

Kira: do you have any idea yet what you'd actually do with it

Student: not really, still thinking. maybe something about the physics of how they spin so long, since i like physics anyway with ms lim. or maybe just how the community around it works, like why so many of us got into it suddenly

Kira: [readback] you got caught playing beyblade in class today expecting to be told off, but mr lim suggested turning it into your SIL project instead, which surprised you since you assumed the project had to be a "serious" topic. you're not sure yet what angle to take, physics of the spin or the social side of why it caught on, but you sound intrigued. fair?

Student: ya fair. need to think more
reflections.validation: You expected a scolding and got an invitation — and instead of shrugging it off, you already have two possible angles for it.
reflections.inferred_meaning: Being told your own interest counts as real learning opened something: the spin physics and the why-did-everyone-get-into-it question are both live.
reflections.story_reframe: Mr Lim caught the Beyblades and, instead of confiscating them, offered you a project. Now you're weighing two directions — the physics of a long spin, or the social side of a sudden craze — and the surprise that play can count as schoolwork hasn't worn off.
reflections.title: Beyblade and the SIL project

reflections.transcript:
Student: eh subject combo deadline coming and i still dont know what to pick

Kira: what's making it hard

Student: like everyone keeps asking me jc or poly and i dont even know. and then within that also need to pick subjects. i just feel lost lah

Kira: if we worked backwards a bit, what would "having it figured out" actually look like for you? like what would you want to be true by the end of this

Student: i guess... knowing what subjects i actually want to study, not just what sounds safe. and maybe having a rough idea why

Kira: ok let's start there then. what have you actually enjoyed so far this year, any subject

Student: physics for sure. ms lim makes it actually make sense, like she always shows us real stuff, not just formulas. i remember the demonstration she did on pressure, that one was so cool

Kira: what specifically did you like about that demo, the doing-it part or seeing how it connects to real life

Student: the real life connection i think. like i remember thinking oh thats why XYZ happens. i like when things click like that

Kira: where else this year have you had that "oh thats why" feeling, even outside class

Student: hmm. actually at the career fair when the physio was talking, i had that feeling too. like he was explaining how the body recovers from injury and i was thinking about my friend joseph who twisted his ankle at camp, and it suddenly made sense why he had to do specific exercises after

Kira: so two very different settings, physics class and a career talk, gave you the same feeling. what do you think that feeling actually is

Student: maybe like... understanding how things work under the surface? not just what happens but why

Kira: that's useful. now what's getting in the way of picking subjects, what's the actual obstacle

Student: i think i dont know if im smart enough for the higher level subjects. and also dont know if jc or poly suits me better, like poly seems more hands on which i think i'd like, but everyone says jc keeps more options open

Kira: when you imagine poly, hands on, what comes to mind

Student: like actually building or doing things, not just sitting and listening. the physio thing, if that was a poly course i feel like there'd be more of that

Kira: and jc, what comes to mind there

Student: more like... still figuring things out broadly before committing. keeping options open like people say

Kira: given everything we've just talked about, the "why things work" feeling, wanting hands on learning, what options are you actually considering right now, doesn't have to be final

Student: i think i want to look more into poly courses related to like sports science or rehab stuff. but also scared to fully rule out jc in case i change my mind

Kira: what would help you feel less scared about that, even a small thing

Student: maybe talking to someone whos actually in a poly course like that. or maybe asking the physio guy from the career fair if i can ask him more questions

Kira: that sounds like a concrete next step. anything else, or is that enough for now

Student: i think thats enough for now. just good to say it out loud actually, didnt realize i already had some idea

Kira: [readback] you came in feeling lost about jc vs poly and subject choices, but tracing back what's actually clicked for you this year, physics with ms lim and the physio's talk at the career fair, you noticed a pattern: you like understanding how things work beneath the surface, and you lean toward hands-on learning. that's pointing you toward poly courses in sports science or rehab, though you're not ready to fully close the door on jc. your next step is trying to talk to someone in that field, maybe even the physio from the career fair. does that capture it

Student: ya that's actually a really good summary. thanks
reflections.validation: You came in lost and talked your way to a pattern — the same 'oh that's why' click in physics class and the physio talk — and even named a next step yourself.
reflections.inferred_meaning: You lean toward understanding how things work under the surface, hands-on — which is quietly pointing at poly sports-science or rehab courses, without closing the JC door.
reflections.story_reframe: The subject-combo deadline had you feeling lost until you traced what actually clicks: Ms Lim's demos, the physio's recovery logic. The pattern you named — why over what, hands-on over listening — points toward poly sports science or rehab, with JC not yet ruled out. Your next step: find someone in that world to ask.
reflections.title: Subject combination decision

vips_pages.compiled_truth: Purpose-linked effort sits at the centre of how you decide: you commit when you can see what the work builds toward (CPR, being ready for your ah gong) and bristle when you can't (foot drills). Contribution shows up as readiness — being the one who knows what to do when someone real needs it. An independence thread is emerging in the subject-combo entry: wanting what you actually want to study, not what sounds safe.
vips_pages.open_question: How do you keep effort meaningful when nobody hands you the why — can you build the habit of finding it yourself?

vips_pages.compiled_truth: A strong Investigative tilt with a hands-on Realistic edge. The repeated signal is the 'oh that's why' feeling: Ms Lim's physics demos, the physio's recovery sequencing, the science behind chip flavours, the physics of a long Beyblade spin. Your interest sustains when understanding connects to something real you can touch or do; an Enterprising thread surfaced unexpectedly in walkathon coordination.
vips_pages.open_question: Which setting keeps the 'oh that's why' feeling alive after the novelty wears off — rehab, applied science, or the operational puzzle of making events run?

vips_pages.compiled_truth: You're usually composed under pressure, with one tested exception — the badge-day freeze under an external examiner — that bothered you precisely because it was out of character. Your decisions lean on trusted peers (NCOC currently rests on Jaya), and you can notice that pattern when it's mirrored back. You're warm and easygoing with friends, slower to open with strangers.
vips_pages.open_question: Can you rebuild trust in your own composure — and find a reason of your own for decisions currently anchored to friends?

vips_pages.compiled_truth: Procedural first aid is real and retained — you still carry the compression rhythm months later. Coordination showed up unexpectedly at walkathon planning: hydration-point logistics across four schools read as problem-solving, not admin. With light scaffolding you name your own patterns — the subject-combo conversation ended with you articulating 'how things work under the surface' yourself.
vips_pages.open_question: Which structured roles let you practise these skills under real conditions — a badge re-attempt with rehearsed scenarios, a named walkathon role, the SIL project?

trajectory.trajectory_text: Your through-line right now isn't simply 'likes physics' or 'likes helping'; it's wanting to understand how things work beneath the surface — and wanting to be ready when it matters for someone real. The strongest evidence points toward hands-on, applied pathways where knowing why translates into doing: healthcare and rehabilitation, applied science, and the operational work of making events happen. The developmental task is to test which of these keeps giving you the 'oh, that's why' feeling once the novelty wears off — and to rebuild trust in your own composure after one bad assessment day.

pathways.label: Sports rehabilitation, nursing and healthcare foundations
pathways.risks_tradeoffs: This route fits your readiness-to-help values and your existing first-aid base, but healthcare pathways are assessment-heavy, and your confidence took a real knock from one external exam. You'd want to check that you're drawn to the slow work of recovery — the exercises, the sequences, the repetition — and not only the rescue moment. One failed badge is a data point, not a verdict.
pathways.exploration_prompt: Set up a conversation with the physiotherapist from the career fair — you already named this as your own next step — and prepare three questions about what the daily work actually involves beyond the cool moments. Treat the badge re-attempt next year as a second experiment: practise the distressed-casualty scenario with a friend playing the casualty, and track whether the freeze was a one-off or something that needs a routine.

pathways.label: Applied physical sciences: how things work beneath the surface
pathways.risks_tradeoffs: Your physics enjoyment is currently teacher-shaped — Ms Lim's real-world demonstrations do a lot of the work. Applied science routes at poly keep the hands-on flavour you want, but the theory load is real, and your 'am I smart enough' doubt needs testing against evidence, not assumption. The SIL project is a low-stakes way to find out before subject combination locks anything in.
pathways.exploration_prompt: Take the physics angle on the Beyblade SIL project: build a simple test rig, change one variable at a time (weight distribution, launch speed, tip shape), and measure spin duration. Track whether the enjoyment survives the measuring and the maths — that's the honest test of whether 'oh, that's why' extends from watching demonstrations to doing the investigation.

pathways.label: Event operations, coordination and enterprise
pathways.risks_tradeoffs: You discovered the organising work was 'genuinely interesting' at the same meeting you developed a crush — the two discoveries are entangled, and the next walkthrough will partly be a test of which one was doing the lifting. Operations and enterprise routes reward your coordination instincts, but sustained admin without a visible mission may drain you the way foot drills do: you need to see what the effort builds toward.
pathways.exploration_prompt: Take a named role in next month's walkathon route walkthrough — own one deliverable end to end, not just help. Then run the class Beyblade interest as a small organised tournament: brackets, rules, a borrowed timer, maybe a five-dollar budget. Afterwards, note which part energised you: the planning, the people, or the problem-solving when something went wrong.

pathways.label: Uniformed leadership: the NCOC track
pathways.risks_tradeoffs: The evidence genuinely cuts both ways. The leadership side appeals ('a level up'), and you've stayed committed through a failed badge — but your strongest recorded frustration is with exactly the discipline-heavy culture NCOC contains more of, and your current deciding rule is 'if she goes i think i go also.' This pathway is here not as a recommendation but as a decision you're already inside of; the experiment is designed to give you a reason of your own either way.
pathways.exploration_prompt: Before the sign-up decision, talk to one current NCOC senior and ask directly: how much of the course is drills and regimentation, and how much is the leadership and real-responsibility work? Then decide with a reason that would still stand if Jaya said no — the decision can include her, but it shouldn't rest on her.

pathways.label: Flavour, fragrance and the chemistry of everyday sensations
pathways.risks_tradeoffs: One moment of surprise is the thinnest evidence base of the five — that's what makes it divergent rather than aligned. The pathway is chemistry-heavy, which you have no recorded signal on yet, positive or negative. Its value here is precisely that: this isn't only about confirming who you already are; it keeps genuinely new doors visibly open, anchored to a real recorded moment rather than invented from nothing.
pathways.exploration_prompt: Visit a polytechnic food science or chemical technology lab during an open house, and try one kitchen-table experiment: blind-taste two brands of the same chip flavour and try to name what differs. If the why behind taste and smell hooks you the way spin physics does, this is a live thread; if not, it cost one afternoon.

open_questions: Which of these directions keeps giving you the 'oh, that's why' feeling once the novelty wears off — the honest test for all five?
Was the badge-day freeze a one-off under an external examiner, or does performing under assessment need a rehearsed routine?
What reason of your own — for or against NCOC — would still stand if Jaya said no?
trajectory.disclaimer: This trajectory is a working hypothesis from your reflections, not a placement decision — it should open conversations with your teachers and family, not close them.

profile.notes_for_review: The seeker. Sharp, internally driven, takes the hard problem on purpose. PCME at H2. Olympiad-track, but slightly disillusioned with the prestige-as-end framing his school culture pushes. The negative reflection (failing an Astro olympiad round) is real grief, not narrative. The 'helping his sister' beat shows he's not purely solitary.

students.coverage_matrix: context_type coverage for demo-b: school (r1, r3, r6), hobby (r2, r5, r8), family (r4), peer (r7). 4 of 5 enum values; ≥3 satisfied. Affect spread: positive (r1, r2, r4), ordinary (r3, r6, r8), negative (r5, r7).

reflections.transcript: H2 Physics today — Mr Goh did the proof for SHM from first principles. The trig substitution at the end was the cleanest thing I've seen all term. Half the class was zoning out but I copied the whole derivation again at home just because I wanted to see if I could do it without the textbook. I could, after three tries. It was past midnight. I didn't notice it was midnight.

Built the eq-platform mount over the weekend, finally. The polar alignment kept drifting because I had the latitude scale 1.3 degrees off — took me until 2am to spot it. Once I corrected it Jupiter sat dead centre for the whole hour I tracked. Took photos of the Galilean moons. Showed Pa the next morning; he asked if I'd been sleeping. I have been sleeping. Just not on the nights when the sky is clear.

Econs essay back. 18 out of 25. Mr Iqbal said my reasoning was 'over-engineered for the marks available.' I get what he means but I also disagree — the simpler answer he wanted misses the actual mechanism. I'll write to the marks scheme next time. But I'm not going to pretend I think the marks scheme is the more honest answer.

Helped Anjali with her Sec 2 Maths. She was stuck on simultaneous equations because they were using the substitution method only and she'd been taught elimination first. I drew both on the same page and she got it in 10 minutes. She said 'why don't teachers do that.' I said because they have 40 of you and one of me. But the honest answer is I think most teachers don't enjoy explaining things twice and I do.

Astro olympiad selection round was today. I got the planetary motion question wrong because I read the problem wrong — confused aphelion with perihelion in the eccentricity formula. The maths was right but on the wrong input. Didn't make the team. I knew within 5 minutes of leaving the hall. I went home and didn't eat dinner. Mum knocked twice. The honest version is: I'm angry not because I lost but because the mistake was the kind of mistake I keep making, and I haven't found the discipline to stop making it.

Maths class today was vectors. The teacher was solving by inspection — guessing the answer and then verifying. I do the same but I don't tell anyone because it feels like cheating. After class I asked Mr Goh whether that's actually how mathematicians work or whether we should learn the formal method first. He said both — the inspection is the hypothesis, the formal method is the proof. That landed. I've been treating the inspection as a shortcut. It's actually the hypothesis step.

Group study at Brandon's place. We were supposed to work through the chem mock. I solved questions 1 to 4 in 20 minutes and the others wanted me to explain how. I tried but I think I went too fast. Daniel said 'bro you're not actually explaining, you're showing off.' That stung because I wasn't trying to show off. I left at 9. The walk back I kept replaying it. Maybe the speed itself is the show-off, even when I don't mean it that way.

Two-hour stretch on the orbital mechanics simulator I'm building in Python. The two-body case works. The three-body case is chaotic and my naive RK4 step is too coarse — it diverges within 10 orbits. I knew the textbook said this. I didn't know what 'chaotic' felt like in my own code until I watched the trajectory smear. Different from reading about it. Adaptive step-size is next.

profile.notes_for_review: The maker. Visual-first, writes and draws compulsively, mistrusts pre-set paths. IP track so no O-levels to anchor against. Tension with family expectation (parents are both doctors) is real but not melodramatic — she still loves them, she just doesn't want what they want for her. The art-block reflection is the negative beat; the 'reading aloud in lit' is the positive.

students.coverage_matrix: context_type coverage for demo-c: hobby (r1, r4, r6), school (r2, r5, r7), family (r3, r8), peer (r9). 4 of 5 enum values; ≥3 satisfied. Affect spread: positive (r2, r4, r7), ordinary (r1, r5, r9), negative (r3, r6, r8).

reflections.transcript: Started a new sketchbook today. Drew the same window from my desk seven times — different times of day. The 6am one is the only one I like. The afternoon ones all feel forced because I was trying to make them good. The 6am one I drew before I was fully awake so I wasn't trying. I want to remember that — the trying is what kills it for me. But I can't just decide to not try.

Mrs Devi asked me to read the Plath poem out loud in Lit today. 'Daddy.' I didn't want to but I did. Halfway through I forgot the rest of the class was there. When I stopped Mrs Devi said 'that's how it's supposed to feel' and didn't say anything else. The class was quiet. I felt exposed but also like I'd done something real. The two feelings sit together.

Dinner argument with Ma again. She asked which CCA I want to commit to for portfolio purposes and I said Art Club. She said 'just Art Club?' as if there was a more serious option I was missing. Pa stayed quiet which is somehow worse than if he'd agreed with her. I went to my room and didn't draw, I just sat. The part that's hard is I don't think she's wrong to worry — the math of it favours her. The part that's hard is I'm still going to do what I'm going to do.

Spent three hours in Bras Basah today. Bought a different kind of ink — sumi, the bottled one from the Kinokuniya stationery floor. Tried it with a stick instead of a brush. The lines were ugly and I loved them. Came back and wrote a paragraph about how the wrong-tool-on-purpose feels like the move I keep making. I don't know if that paragraph is for anyone or just for me. Probably just for me.

Chemistry today. Titration. I followed every step and got the right concentration to two decimal places. Mr Pereira said well done. I wasn't happy. There was nothing in it that was mine. I knew the answer before I started because we'd been told what the answer should be. I don't want to do this for a living. I'm sure that's not new information to me but today it landed differently.

Tried to start the next piece for the school art show. Sat at my desk for 90 minutes. Could not start. Every line felt like a commitment I wasn't ready to make. I ended up doodling the same vine pattern in the corner over and over. Eventually I put the paper away. I'm scared the show is going to be the first time I make work that I have to make rather than work that I want to make. Maybe that's just being a grown-up artist. Maybe it's a warning.

Lit lesson on close-reading today. Mrs Devi gave us four lines from a Mary Oliver and asked us to write 300 words. I wrote 800. I couldn't stop. The way the line break sits between 'wild' and 'precious' — there's a whole essay in just that space. I handed in 300 and saved the rest. The 500 I cut were the ones I cared about most. That's the second time this week I've noticed: the work I do for myself is bigger than the work I hand in.

Pa took me to his clinic on Saturday morning. He said he wasn't trying to recruit me, he just wanted me to see what a day looks like. I watched him with a young couple whose baby had a rash. He was good with them. He was patient in a way he sometimes isn't with me. I came home and didn't know what to do with that. He was right that it changed how I see him. He was also right that it didn't change what I want.

Showed Hannah the sketchbook today. She flipped through it for ten minutes without saying anything. I almost said 'never mind' three times. At the end she said 'the 6am window one is the best.' That's the same one I'd marked. We didn't talk much after that but I felt seen in a way I don't usually feel seen at school. I want to remember her name in this notebook so future-me knows who saw me first.

profile.name_handle: Aaron (Sec 4, IP transferring to poly route)
profile.notes_for_review: The steady. Hands-on, finishes things, is the one peers ask to make things work. Considering polytechnic (likely SP Mechanical Engineering) over JC despite the IP school's strong-default-to-JC culture. The reflections show the slow shape of him noticing his own pattern: he is happiest when something concrete needs doing for someone he likes. The negative beat is the relay race injury — physical setback that exposes the tightness of his self-image.

students.coverage_matrix: context_type coverage for demo-d: school (r1, r5), hobby (r2, r4, r7), peer (r3), family (r6). 4 of 5 enum values; ≥3 satisfied. Affect spread: positive (r1, r3, r6), ordinary (r5, r7), negative (r2, r4).

reflections.transcript: Robotics CCA today. The Sec 1s were jamming on the chassis assembly — they kept stripping the M3 screws because they were over-torquing with the wrong driver. I sat with Faiz for 20 minutes and just showed him the half-turn-and-stop trick my brother taught me. By the end he was teaching the other two. Mr Selvam said 'you're the only senior they actually listen to.' That hit me funny because I'm not the smartest one in the room. I just don't make them feel stupid.

Pulled my hamstring at relay training today. The MO said two weeks off. I sat through the rest of the session watching from the bench and I hated it more than I expected. Not because of the race — the race I can win or lose, doesn't matter. It's because the team can't pass batons properly without the four of us actually running together. They were trying and they were failing and I couldn't help. I went home and iced it and pretended to be okay at dinner.

Helped Jia Hao move into his new place at Hougang on Sunday. His family sold the old flat. He didn't ask anyone — I just heard from his cousin and showed up at 8am with a borrowed trolley. We were done by 2pm. He didn't say much. On the MRT back he said 'eh thanks ah' and I said 'no need' and we left it. That was enough for both of us. I think that's what 'enough' looks like for me — small actions, no speech.

Tried to fix Ma's broken kitchen tap myself before calling a plumber. Watched two YouTube videos. The cartridge was different from the videos. I broke the sink stopper trying to lever the old cartridge out. Plumber came and fixed it and charged 80. Ma laughed about it later but in the moment I felt small. I usually finish what I start. I don't usually fail in front of my own house.

Subject combo briefing today. Mrs Lim laid out the JC routes and a 10-minute slide at the end on poly. The poly slide was thinner. I asked her after class about SP Mechanical Engineering specifically. She was honest — said it's a strong programme but I'd be walking away from the IP advantage. I asked what advantage exactly and she said 'the keep-options-open advantage.' I left thinking — I don't actually want all the options open. I want one that fits.

Talked to Kor Kor about the poly decision. He did the JC route and he's now in NTU doing biz. He didn't try to talk me out of it — he just asked what my day-by-day would look like if I picked poly vs JC. I told him. He said 'the poly day sounds like you and the JC day sounds like me.' That's the first time anyone's said it that clearly. Ma still doesn't know I'm seriously thinking about poly. Telling her is the part I keep putting off.

Back at training after the two weeks. Hamstring held. I ran the 200 split slower than my PB but the baton pass with Idris was clean — we'd been doing pickups together for the whole break, off the track, just walking through the motion. Coach Yusof said the pass looked like we'd been running it for a year. We have, in a way. I don't think the speed is what makes the relay work. I think it's the handover.
